---
title: "35 · QAC: The Simplest Actor-Critic"
subtitle: "Learn q via TD (the critic) and use it inside the policy gradient (the actor)."
lesson-id: "mfrl.actor-critic-qac"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.reinforce"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Can you state **REINFORCE** ([Lesson 34](../34-reinforce/index.qmd))?
2. Do you know the **Sarsa** update — TD on $q$ for the on-policy case
   ([Lesson 24](../24-sarsa/index.qmd))?
3. Are you comfortable with **semi-gradient** TD with function approximation
   ([Lesson 29](../29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd))?

If yes to all three, continue.
:::

## Touch

Picture a split window. **Left pane:** a small grid-world with a policy
$\pi_\theta$ — coloured arrows show how likely each action is at each cell.
**Right pane:** the action-value table $\hat q(s, a; w)$, shaded by value.

Click "step". Two things happen:

1. The **critic** updates $w$ from one TD-style observation: it nudges
   $\hat q(s, a; w)$ toward $r + \gamma \hat q(s', a'; w)$.
2. The **actor** then updates $\theta$ using the *current* $\hat q(s, a; w)$
   in place of the unknown $q_\pi(s, a)$:
   $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\pi \nabla \log \pi(a \mid s; \theta)
   \hat q(s, a; w)$.

After many clicks, both panes evolve together — the critic learns the
policy's $q$; the actor improves the policy.

::: {.lesson-beat}
Two learners, one feedback loop. The critic supplies the actor's $q$
estimate; the actor changes the policy the critic must evaluate.
:::

## Wonder

1. REINFORCE used the *return* $G_t$ as an unbiased estimate of $q_\pi$.
   Why might a learned $\hat q(s, a; w)$ be preferable?
2. The critic updates $\hat q$ using TD bootstrapping — biased but
   low-variance. The actor uses $\hat q$ as if it were correct. Won't the
   bias hurt?
3. Both networks are learning *simultaneously*. Why doesn't this loop spin
   out of control?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — what does the actor really need?

The policy gradient theorem says
$\nabla J = \mathbb E_{(s,a) \sim \pi}[\nabla \log \pi(a \mid s)\, q_\pi(s, a)]$.
What's the cheapest way to estimate $q_\pi(s, a)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You have two candidates already in your toolkit: the MC return $G_t$
(REINFORCE) and the TD bootstrap target.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two natural estimators:

- **MC return $G_t$** — unbiased, high variance, requires waiting for the
  episode to finish (REINFORCE).
- **TD estimate $\hat q(s, a; w)$** — biased but lower variance, available
  immediately after the next transition.

The trade is the same one you saw in Lesson 27: bias for variance. A
*learned $\hat q$* is the actor-critic answer.
</details>

### Problem 2 — train the critic with Sarsa

Let $\hat q(s, a; w)$ be the critic, parameterised. On a transition
$(s, a, r, s', a')$ where $a' \sim \pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s')$ — i.e.,
**on-policy** — write the Sarsa-style critic update.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Reuse semi-gradient TD from Lesson 29 with the next-action sampled by the
current policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$w \leftarrow w + \alpha_w\,\bigl[r + \gamma \hat q(s', a'; w) - \hat q(s, a; w)\bigr]\, \nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w).$$

This is Sarsa with FA. Because $a'$ is drawn under $\pi_\theta$, the
critic learns the *current* policy's $q$ — exactly what the actor needs.
</details>

### Problem 3 — actor update with $\hat q$

The actor's update follows REINFORCE but replaces $G_t$ by the critic's
estimate $\hat q(s, a; w)$:

$$\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\theta\,\nabla_\theta \log \pi(a \mid s; \theta)\, \hat q(s, a; w).$$

Why is this *biased* with respect to $\nabla J$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\hat q(s, a; w) \ne q_{\pi_\theta}(s, a)$ in general — the critic is
itself an approximation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\hat q(s, a; w)$ is a learned approximation to $q_\pi$ — biased
(non-zero approximation error) and itself moving (Sarsa is bootstrapping
its own targets). Substituting it for $q_\pi$ in the policy gradient
theorem produces a biased gradient. The hope: lower variance more than
makes up for the bias. In practice, on most tasks, it does.
</details>

### Problem 4 — name the two roles

Two parameterised functions, two updates per step. Name them.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- **Actor.** The policy $\pi_\theta(a \mid s)$ — what the agent does.
- **Critic.** The value function $\hat q(s, a; w)$ — what the agent
  thinks each $(s, a)$ is worth.

The actor proposes; the critic evaluates. The critic learns the actor's
current value. The actor improves itself using the critic's evaluation.
The cycle is the **actor-critic** pattern.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The "actor-critic" name is older than Q-learning. **Barto, Sutton &
Anderson (1983)** introduced it in their pole-balancing paper, where two
neural networks — one outputting actions ("ACE", the actor) and one
predicting reinforcement ("ACE-2", the critic) — split the policy and
value roles. The terminology has stuck for forty-three years and counting.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
**QAC is REINFORCE with TD($q$) replacing the MC return.** That's the
entire idea. Same actor update, just a faster (biased) substitute for
$q_\pi$. Every later actor-critic method differs only in *which estimator
of $q_\pi$ (or $A_\pi$) the actor uses.*
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**QAC (Q Actor-Critic).** Maintain two parameterised functions and update
them in tandem on each transition $(s, a, r, s', a')$ with $a' \sim
\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s')$:

- **Critic (Sarsa with FA):**
  $w \leftarrow w + \alpha_w[r + \gamma \hat q(s', a'; w) - \hat q(s, a; w)]\nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w)$.
- **Actor (PG with critic's $\hat q$):**
  $\theta \leftarrow \theta + \alpha_\theta \nabla_\theta \log \pi(a \mid s; \theta)\,\hat q(s, a; w)$.

The critic estimates the *current* $q_{\pi_\theta}$; the actor consumes
the critic's estimate inside the policy gradient.

:::

A mnemonic:

> *Critic learns; actor steers.*

## Apply

### Worked example — pseudo-code for QAC

```
Initialise θ, w arbitrarily; choose α_w, α_θ.
Sample initial state s and action a ~ π_θ(·|s).
Repeat:
    Take action a; observe reward r and next state s'.
    Sample next action a' ~ π_θ(·|s').
    δ ← r + γ q̂(s', a'; w) - q̂(s, a; w)        ← TD error
    w ← w + α_w · δ · ∇_w q̂(s, a; w)              ← critic update (Sarsa)
    θ ← θ + α_θ · ∇_θ log π(a|s; θ) · q̂(s, a; w) ← actor update
    s ← s'; a ← a'.
```

Two updates per transition. The critic learns the current policy's $q$;
the actor uses it.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** What estimator of $q_\pi$ does QAC use?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A learned, parameterised $\hat q(s, a; w)$ trained by Sarsa-style TD on
the current policy's transitions.
</details>

**W2.** Is QAC on-policy or off-policy?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sarsa samples $a' \sim \pi_\theta$. So both data and target use the same
policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**On-policy.** The data $(s, a, r, s', a')$ is generated by $\pi_\theta$,
and the critic uses $a' \sim \pi_\theta$. (Off-policy actor-critic comes in
[Lesson 37](../37-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd).)
</details>

**W3.** What is biased and what is unbiased about QAC?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The actor's gradient is **biased** because $\hat q$ approximates $q_\pi$
(approximation error + bootstrap bias). The score-function form of the
gradient identity itself is exact. Variance of the actor's gradient is
typically **lower** than REINFORCE's because $\hat q$ is much smoother
than the random return $G_t$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** What happens if you set the critic's step size $\alpha_w$ very
small relative to the actor's $\alpha_\theta$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The critic stops tracking the changing policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The critic lags far behind the policy; $\hat q$ is the value of an
out-of-date $\pi$. The actor's gradients become more biased and the loop
can fail to improve. The standard rule of thumb is $\alpha_w \gtrsim
\alpha_\theta$ — the critic should learn at least as fast as the actor
moves.
</details>

**P2.** What goes wrong if you reverse the rule and set $\alpha_w$ much
*larger* than $\alpha_\theta$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Could the critic itself become unstable?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two failure modes. First, the critic's TD update with a large step can
oscillate or diverge under FA (Lessons 29–30). Second, even if it
converges, $\hat q$ is computed against a fixed policy that's barely
moving — fine in principle but wasteful (the critic is doing much more
work than it has to). The good regime is moderate $\alpha_w \approx
2\text{–}10 \times \alpha_\theta$.
</details>

**P3.** Modify the QAC pseudo-code to use **Q-learning** for the critic
(i.e., bootstrap with $\max_{a''} \hat q(s', a''; w)$). Is the resulting
algorithm still on-policy?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Q-learning's $\max$ doesn't follow the policy.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The critic update becomes
$w \leftarrow w + \alpha_w[r + \gamma \max_{a''} \hat q(s', a''; w) - \hat
q(s, a; w)]\nabla_w \hat q(s, a; w)$.
This is **off-policy** (the bootstrap target uses the greedy action, not the
on-policy one). The actor still uses $\hat q$ from this critic. The
construction works but flirts with the [deadly triad](/glossary.qmd) (Lesson
30) — bootstrapping + off-policy + FA. In practice such variants are stable
on small tasks but not robust on large ones.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Compare QAC's update rule against REINFORCE-with-baseline. What
condition would make them identical in expectation?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
REINFORCE-with-baseline subtracts $b(s)$ from $G_t$. QAC plugs in $\hat q$
without subtracting. What if $b = 0$ and $G_t = \hat q$ in expectation?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
If $\mathbb E[G_t \mid s_t, a_t] = \hat q(s_t, a_t; w)$ (i.e., $\hat q$ is a
perfect estimator of $q_\pi$) and there is no baseline, QAC and REINFORCE
have the same expected gradient. They differ only in *variance*: QAC's
gradient is much less noisy because $\hat q$ is a smooth function instead
of a random return. (Adding a baseline takes you to A2C — next lesson.)
</details>

**S2.** Why doesn't the actor-critic loop spin out of control even though
both functions are changing simultaneously?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Two timescales: the critic learns fast against a slowly-changing target.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The convergence theory of two-timescale stochastic approximation (Borkar
1997) tells us: if $\alpha_w / \alpha_\theta \to \infty$, then on the
critic's faster timescale the policy looks frozen, so the critic
converges to $q_{\pi_\theta}$ for the current $\theta$. On the actor's
slower timescale, the actor sees an essentially-correct $\hat q$ and does
clean policy gradient ascent. With both step sizes finite (as in
practice), the dynamics are not theoretically guaranteed to converge but
work in most cases. This is the same theory underlying [Robbins–Monro](../20-robbins-monro/index.qmd).
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Actor-critic is two algorithms running in parallel."** It's better to
   think of it as one algorithm with two coupled updates. The critic's
   target depends on the actor; the actor's gradient depends on the critic.
2. **"QAC is just Sarsa for the policy."** No — Sarsa updates a value
   *table or function*; QAC updates a *parameterised policy* using
   Sarsa's value estimates. The mechanism is different.
3. **"You always need a baseline in actor-critic."** No — QAC has no
   baseline. A2C ([Lesson 36](../36-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd))
   adds one and gets a major variance reduction. QAC is the foundation.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **QAC = REINFORCE with $\hat q$ replacing $G_t$.** Lower variance,
   non-zero bias. The critic gets trained by Sarsa.
2. **Critic learns the policy's $q$; actor uses it for the policy
   gradient.** Two timescales, one feedback loop.
3. Every later actor-critic method (A2C, off-policy AC, DDPG) differs
   from QAC only in *which* estimator of $q_\pi$ or $A_\pi$ goes inside
   the actor's update.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you sketch the QAC pseudo-code from memory?
2. Did you derive the actor update by substituting $\hat q$ into the PG
   theorem yourself?
3. Could you explain the bias-variance trade vs REINFORCE in one
   sentence?

- **3 yes:** continue to [Lesson 36 — A2C](../36-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd).
- **2 yes:** redo P1 and P2.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[mfrl.reinforce](../34-reinforce/index.qmd)** — the policy-gradient
  update with the MC return as the $q$-estimator.
- **[mfrl.sarsa](../24-sarsa/index.qmd)** — the on-policy TD update used
  by the critic.

### What this unlocks

- **[A2C](../36-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd)** — replace $\hat q$ by
  the *advantage* $A = q - v$ for major variance reduction.
- **[Off-policy AC](../37-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd)** — use a
  behaviour policy different from the target.
- **[Deterministic AC (DDPG)](../38-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd)** —
  the deterministic-policy version of the same skeleton.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Almost every modern policy-based agent is some flavour of
actor-critic: A2C, A3C, PPO, IMPALA, SAC, TD3, DDPG. The split between
"learn what to do" (actor) and "learn what's worth doing" (critic) maps
neatly onto how complex agents are typically organised. RLHF for LLM
training is also an actor-critic system: the LLM is the actor, the
reward model is the critic.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning that this
track expands into 38 lessons.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §10 (actor-critic). Original reference: Barto, Sutton
  & Anderson (1983), *Neuronlike adaptive elements that can solve
  difficult learning control problems*, IEEE TSMC 13.
- Touchstones honoured: Russian school (Discover walks the learner from
  REINFORCE's MC estimator to a TD substitute before *Name*); Better
  Explained "Aha!" framing (the key insight that QAC = REINFORCE with $\hat
  q$).
:::
